topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper felt like sandpaper under his fingertips as he unfolded the document. Every carefully chosen adjective, every carefully framed anecdote, was a lie. His name, his triumphs, his supposed struggles—all meticulously constructed by someone else. A slow, creeping cold began to spread through his chest, tightening his throat until it felt impossible to draw a breath. He reread the section detailing his early career, the descriptions of his 'visionary spark,' and a wave of nausea washed over him. He dropped the paper onto the polished wood floor with a soft thud, the sound echoing loudly in the silent study. His hands began to tremble uncontrollably, and he instinctively pressed them against his eyes, trying to block out the stark reality laid bare before him. He stood up, swaying slightly, needing to move, needing to escape the suffocating weight of the fabricated life.

[story 2]

"This is absurd," she whispered, the sound barely audible above the hum of the city outside. Elias stared at the printed pages, the ink seeming to mock him with its perfect, calculated elegance. He traced a line with a hesitant finger, his jaw clenching so tightly the muscles in his neck stood out like cords. The air in the cramped apartment suddenly felt thin, suffocating. He felt a flush rise from his collarbone, spreading rapidly across his cheeks, leaving him feeling exposed and intensely vulnerable. He looked around the room, suddenly seeing every shadow as an accusation, every familiar object as a testament to the elaborate deception. He swallowed hard, the dryness in his mouth making the action painful, and finally, he crumpled the paper into a tight, useless ball, his knuckles white against the crumpled edges.

[story 3]

The silence in the archive room was heavy, broken only by the faint whir of the ventilation system. When he finally recognized the signature—a name that wasn't his—a profound sense of disorientation hit him, followed by a dizzying wave of shame. He leaned back against the cool metal shelving, his posture collapsing inward as if trying to shrink away from the revelation. His breath hitched, shallow and rapid, and he could feel the heat radiating off his skin. He lifted a hand, not to touch the documents again, but simply to press it against his forehead, as if trying to physically push the knowledge away. Every muscle in his body seemed to lock up; his legs felt unsteady, and he had to consciously force himself to take a deep, measured breath, the effort costing him dearly.
